---
title: "Stochastic Processes"
description: "Randomness evolving over time through Markov chains, martingales, counting processes, Brownian motion, SDEs, and MCMC-facing long-run behavior."
page-type: module-overview
status: published
update-status: current
level: advanced
topic: stochastic-processes
prerequisites:
  - /topics/foundation/probability/index.qmd
  - /topics/foundation/linear-algebra/index.qmd
learning-goals:
  - "Understand why stochastic processes add time, dependence, and long-run behavior to ordinary probability."
  - "Know the current first-pass order for the stochastic-processes module."
  - "Connect Markov chains, martingales, diffusions, and MCMC-style ideas to adjacent modules on control, signal processing, and modern ML."
applications:
  - stochastic-processes
  - engineering-systems
  - machine-learning-theory
keywords:
  - stochastic processes
  - Markov chains
  - martingales
  - Brownian motion
  - SDEs
maintainer: mtuann
last-reviewed: 2026-04-26
source-check-date: 2026-04-26
review-cadence: annual
date-modified: last-modified
draft: false
stable-sources:
  - "MIT 18.445 Introduction to Stochastic Processes"
  - "MIT 15.070J Advanced Stochastic Processes"
  - "MIT 6.262 Discrete Stochastic Processes"
  - "Stanford Stats 218 Introduction to Stochastic Processes II"
  - "Stanford Stats366 course notes"
  - "Stanford Stats 310C Theory of Probability"
current-sources:
  - "MIT 18.445 lecture notes page"
  - "MIT 18.445 lecture 2"
  - "MIT 18.445 lecture 15"
  - "MIT 18.445 lecture 16"
  - "MIT 18.445 lecture 4"
  - "MIT 6.262 course page"
  - "MIT 6.262 chapter 5 resource"
  - "MIT 15.070J lecture notes page"
  - "MIT 15.070J lecture 10"
  - "Stanford Stats366 Markov chains notes"
  - "Stanford Stats 218 course page"
  - "Stanford Stats 310C syllabus"
  - "Stanford MS&E 221 Stochastic Modeling"
---

## Why This Module Matters

Classical probability teaches random variables, conditioning, expectations, concentration, and a few asymptotic theorems.

Stochastic processes ask a different kind of question:

- what if randomness evolves over time?
- what if the present depends on the past?
- what if long-run behavior matters more than one-step uncertainty?
- what if the right object is not one random variable, but an entire random path?

That is why stochastic-process language keeps reappearing in:

- queues and networks
- hidden-state models and filtering
- stochastic control and RL
- Brownian motion and diffusion
- MCMC and sampling algorithms

This module is the bridge from ordinary probability into time-indexed randomness.

::: {.meta-strip}
::: {.meta-box}
**Prerequisites**
[Probability](../../foundation/probability/index.qmd) should come first. [Linear Algebra](../../foundation/linear-algebra/index.qmd) helps because transition operators and stationary distributions are often matrix objects. [Real Analysis](../real-analysis/index.qmd) helps later once paths, limits, and continuous-time objects become more explicit.
:::
::: {.meta-box}
**Unlocks**
Markov chains, martingales, Poisson processes, Brownian motion, SDEs, long-run behavior, and MCMC bridges
:::
::: {.meta-box}
**Research Use**
Reading papers or courses on stochastic control, sequential inference, diffusion models, MCMC, random walks, and stochastic modeling
:::
:::

## First Pass Through This Module

The current first-pass spine for this module is:

1. [Markov Chains and Stationary Distributions](concepts/markov-chains-and-stationary-distributions.qmd)
2. [Martingales and Optional Stopping Intuition](concepts/martingales-and-optional-stopping-intuition.qmd)
3. [Poisson Processes and Counting Models](concepts/poisson-processes-and-counting-models.qmd)
4. [Brownian Motion and Diffusion Intuition](concepts/brownian-motion-and-diffusion-intuition.qmd)
5. [SDEs and Ito Intuition](concepts/sdes-and-ito-intuition.qmd)
6. [Mixing, Ergodicity, and MCMC Bridges](concepts/mixing-ergodicity-and-mcmc-bridges.qmd)

The full first-pass spine is now live, from [Markov Chains and Stationary Distributions](concepts/markov-chains-and-stationary-distributions.qmd) through [Martingales and Optional Stopping Intuition](concepts/martingales-and-optional-stopping-intuition.qmd), [Poisson Processes and Counting Models](concepts/poisson-processes-and-counting-models.qmd), [Brownian Motion and Diffusion Intuition](concepts/brownian-motion-and-diffusion-intuition.qmd), [SDEs and Ito Intuition](concepts/sdes-and-ito-intuition.qmd), and [Mixing, Ergodicity, and MCMC Bridges](concepts/mixing-ergodicity-and-mcmc-bridges.qmd).

## How To Use This Module

The best current first-pass path is:

1. start with [Markov Chains and Stationary Distributions](concepts/markov-chains-and-stationary-distributions.qmd)
2. read [Martingales and Optional Stopping Intuition](concepts/martingales-and-optional-stopping-intuition.qmd) when conditional expectation, stopping rules, or "fairness given current information" becomes the main idea
3. continue to [Poisson Processes and Counting Models](concepts/poisson-processes-and-counting-models.qmd) and [Brownian Motion and Diffusion Intuition](concepts/brownian-motion-and-diffusion-intuition.qmd) if you want the two main continuous-time randomness lenses: jumps and diffusion
4. use [SDEs and Ito Intuition](concepts/sdes-and-ito-intuition.qmd) once drift-plus-noise language starts appearing in control or diffusion-style ML
5. use [Mixing, Ergodicity, and MCMC Bridges](concepts/mixing-ergodicity-and-mcmc-bridges.qmd) once long-run sampling, dependent averages, or MCMC becomes the main question
6. keep [Probability](../../foundation/probability/index.qmd) and [Linear Algebra](../../foundation/linear-algebra/index.qmd) nearby whenever conditioning, expectation, transition operators, or stationary distributions need re-grounding

The design goal is to make stochastic evolution itself feel natural before the module branches into martingales, diffusions, and MCMC-like long-run behavior.

## Core Concepts

- [Markov Chains and Stationary Distributions](concepts/markov-chains-and-stationary-distributions.qmd): the opening page that explains the Markov property, transition matrices, stationary distributions, and first-pass long-run behavior.
- [Martingales and Optional Stopping Intuition](concepts/martingales-and-optional-stopping-intuition.qmd): the live second page where stochastic fairness, conditional expectation, and stopping ideas become reusable.
- [Poisson Processes and Counting Models](concepts/poisson-processes-and-counting-models.qmd): the live counting-process page where random event streams, independent increments, and exponential waiting times appear.
- [Brownian Motion and Diffusion Intuition](concepts/brownian-motion-and-diffusion-intuition.qmd): the live continuous-time randomness page where Gaussian increments, diffusion scaling, and path roughness become central.
- [SDEs and Ito Intuition](concepts/sdes-and-ito-intuition.qmd): the live fifth page where drift-diffusion models, Ito corrections, and Euler-Maruyama simulation become explicit.
- [Mixing, Ergodicity, and MCMC Bridges](concepts/mixing-ergodicity-and-mcmc-bridges.qmd): the live sixth page where long-run convergence, ergodic averages, and MCMC-style sampling viewpoints become central.

## Proof Patterns In This Module

- `One-step to long-run`: use a local transition rule to study global behavior over many steps.
- `Conditional expectation as structure`: replace pathwise complexity with cleaner conditional objects.
- `Invariant or stationary objects`: identify distributions or functionals that remain stable under the dynamics.

## Applications

### Random Dynamics Over Time

The module organizes systems where randomness is not one-shot but evolves step by step or continuously over time.

### Sequential Inference And Hidden State

Markov structure is the natural backbone for filtering, HMM-style reasoning, and many latent-state models.

### Control, RL, Diffusion, And MCMC

This is the language that later supports Markov decision processes, stochastic control, diffusion modeling, and sampling algorithms.

## Go Deeper By Topic

The strongest adjacent live pages right now are:

- [Probability](../../foundation/probability/index.qmd)
- [Stochastic Control and Dynamic Programming](../stochastic-control-and-dynamic-programming/index.qmd)
- [State Estimation, Smoothing, and Hidden-State Inference](../signal-processing-and-estimation/concepts/state-estimation-smoothing-and-hidden-state-inference.qmd)
- [Score Matching and the SDE View of Diffusion](../../../applications/machine-learning/score-matching-and-the-sde-view-of-diffusion.qmd)
- [Continuous-Time Stochastic Control and Hamilton-Jacobi-Bellman Intuition](../stochastic-control-and-dynamic-programming/concepts/continuous-time-stochastic-control-and-hamilton-jacobi-bellman-intuition.qmd)

## Optional Deeper Reading After First Pass

The strongest current references connected to this module are:

- [MIT 18.445 lecture notes page](https://ocw.mit.edu/courses/18-445-introduction-to-stochastic-processes-spring-2015/pages/lecture-notes) - official MIT route through Markov chains, martingales, mixing, Poisson processes, and beyond. Checked `2026-04-25`.
- [MIT 6.262 Discrete Stochastic Processes](https://ocw.mit.edu/courses/6-262-discrete-stochastic-processes-spring-2011/) - official MIT course hub for discrete-time chains, recurrence, and countable-state behavior. Checked `2026-04-25`.
- [Stanford Stats 218](https://web.stanford.edu/class/stats218/stats218.html) - official Stanford stochastic-processes page with martingales and diffusion-facing topics. Checked `2026-04-25`.
- [Stanford MS&E 221](https://web.stanford.edu/class/msande221/) - official Stanford stochastic-modeling course page covering discrete- and continuous-time Markov chains and renewal-style models. Checked `2026-04-25`.

## Sources and Further Reading

- [MIT 18.445 lecture notes page](https://ocw.mit.edu/courses/18-445-introduction-to-stochastic-processes-spring-2015/pages/lecture-notes) - `First pass` - official MIT lecture hub for a broad first route through stochastic processes. Checked `2026-04-25`.
- [MIT 18.445 lecture 2](https://ocw.mit.edu/courses/18-445-introduction-to-stochastic-processes-spring-2015/2edaa8542469ce692226813a8bd1ec88_MIT18_445S15_lecture2.pdf) - `First pass` - official MIT note on Markov chains and stationary distributions. Checked `2026-04-25`.
- [MIT 6.262 Discrete Stochastic Processes](https://ocw.mit.edu/courses/6-262-discrete-stochastic-processes-spring-2011/) - `Second pass` - official MIT course hub for discrete stochastic-process language. Checked `2026-04-25`.
- [MIT 6.262 chapter 5 resource](https://ocw.mit.edu/courses/6-262-discrete-stochastic-processes-spring-2011/resources/mit6_262s11_chap05/) - `Second pass` - official MIT chapter resource for countable-state Markov chains. Checked `2026-04-25`.
- [Stanford Stats366 Markov chains notes](https://web.stanford.edu/class/stats366/exs/Markov1.html) - `Second pass` - useful Stanford note for a concise first-pass view of discrete-time chains and stationary behavior. Checked `2026-04-25`.
- [Stanford Stats 218](https://web.stanford.edu/class/stats218/stats218.html) - `Bridge outward` - useful Stanford page once the module broadens into martingales and diffusions. Checked `2026-04-25`.
- [Stanford MS&E 221](https://web.stanford.edu/class/msande221/) - `Bridge outward` - useful Stanford stochastic-modeling anchor once discrete and continuous-time models are both in play. Checked `2026-04-25`.
